title: What are closures in JavaScript?
answer:
**Core concept.** A *closure* is a function bundled together with references to the variables of the scope in which it was **defined** — its lexical environment. The inner function keeps a *live link* to those outer variables even after the outer function has already returned.
**How it works.** When a function is created, the engine attaches a hidden reference to the scope it was born in. Variable lookups walk *outward* along this chain. Because the link is to the **variable itself** (not a copy of its value), every call sees the latest value, and the scope is kept on the heap for as long as the inner function is reachable — which is why state outlives the outer call. ### Where closures show up | Pattern | What the closure captures | | --- | --- | | Data privacy / module pattern | private state no caller can reach | | Function factories | configuration baked into the returned fn | | Memoization / caching | a cache that persists across calls | | Event handlers & callbacks | the surrounding variables at definition time | > **Interview tip:** Say explicitly that a closure captures a *reference to the variable*, not a snapshot of its value. That one sentence explains the classic var-in-a-for-loop bug (every callback logs the final value) and why a per-iteration let binding fixes it.

examples.label: Private state + the loop-capture gotcha

title: What are computed property names?
answer:
**Core concept.** Computed property names let you use an **expression** — wrapped in square brackets — as an object (or class) key inside the literal itself. The expression is evaluated at creation time and its result becomes the key, so you can build dynamic keys without a separate assignment step.
**How it works.** Before ES2015 you had to create the object and then assign a dynamic key with bracket notation. Computed keys fold that into the literal: { [expr]: value }. The expression can be a variable, a template string, a function call, or a Symbol — which is exactly how you attach well-known symbols like [Symbol.iterator] directly in a literal or class body. ### Where they help | Use | Example | | --- | --- | | Dynamic key from a variable | { [field]: value } | | Built key from a template | { [`item_${id}`]: data } | | Symbol keys | { [Symbol.iterator]() {} } | | Reducers / grouping | build a lookup map by key | > **Interview tip:** Pair this with the ES2015 *shorthand* — { name } for { name: name } — as the two object-literal upgrades. A clean use case is turning an array into a keyed lookup with reduce and a computed key.

examples.label: Dynamic keys in a literal

title: What are modules in JavaScript?
answer:
**Core concept.** Modules let you split code into separate files that explicitly **export** values and **import** them elsewhere. Each ES module has its **own scope** (no global pollution), runs **once** (cached as a singleton), is always in **strict mode**, and loads **deferred**.
**How it works.** You expose values with **named** exports (export const x) and/or one **default** export per file, and pull them in with import. Because each module is evaluated once and its exports are cached, modules act as singletons — great for shared config/state. Imports are *live bindings* (you see later updates to an exported let), and you can lazy-load on demand with the async import() function for code-splitting. In the browser you opt in with <script type="module">. ### Module mechanics | Feature | Behaviour | | --- | --- | | Scope | per-file (no globals) | | Exports | named + one default | | Evaluation | once, cached (singleton) | | Mode | always strict | | Dynamic load | await import("./x.js") | > **Interview tip:** Note that imports are **live read-only bindings** (not copies), modules run in **strict mode** automatically, and import() enables lazy code-splitting. Contrast static import (hoisted, top-level) with dynamic import() (a promise, anywhere).

examples.label: named, default, and dynamic import

title: What are private class fields?
answer:
**Core concept.** Private class fields are members prefixed with # that are accessible **only inside the class body** — privacy enforced by the language itself, not a naming convention like a leading underscore. There are private fields (#count), private methods (#helper()), and static private members.
**How it works.** Unlike the old _underscore convention (which is just a hint — the property is still public), # fields are **inaccessible** from outside; even attempting obj.#x in other code is a *syntax* error. They don't appear in Object.keys, JSON.stringify, or for…in, giving real encapsulation. You can also write #method() for private helpers and static #field for class-level secrets, and use #x in obj as a private brand-check. ### Privacy options | Form | Privacy | | --- | --- | | _name | convention only (still public) | | #name | hard private (enforced) | | #method() | private method | | static #x | private static member | > **Interview tip:** Contrast hard # privacy with the _ convention and closures (the pre-class way to get privacy). Note # fields are invisible to JSON.stringify/Object.keys — true encapsulation.

examples.label: Hard-private state in a class

title: What are static methods and properties?
answer:
**Core concept.** static members belong to the **class itself**, not to instances. You call them as ClassName.method() / read ClassName.prop — they're for utilities, factory functions, and constants that don't need any particular object's state.
**How it works.** A static method lives on the constructor function, so it isn't on the prototype and instances don't inherit it (new MathUtil().square is undefined). Inside a static method, this refers to the **class**, which is why static *factory* methods (User.fromJSON(...)) can call new this(...). Built-ins use this pattern everywhere: Array.isArray, Object.keys, Math.max, Promise.all. There are also static blocks (static { ... }) for complex one-time class setup. ### static vs instance | | static | instance | | --- | --- | --- | | Lives on | the class | the prototype/object | | Called via | Class.fn() | obj.fn() | | this | the class | the instance | | Use for | utilities, factories, constants | per-object behaviour | > **Interview tip:** Cite real examples (Array.isArray, Promise.all) and the **factory** pattern (static fromJSON returning new this(...)). Stress that instances don't see static members.

examples.label: Class-level utilities + factory

title: What are tagged template literals?
answer:
**Core concept.** A *tag* is a function placed directly before a template literal. Instead of building a string, JavaScript calls the function with the **static string parts** (as an array) and the **interpolated values** (as the rest of the arguments) — letting the function process and return *anything*.
**How it works.** For tag`a${x}b`, the engine calls tag(["a","b"], x) — the strings array always has exactly one more element than the values. Because the tag controls the output, it can sanitize/escape interpolations (preventing injection), build CSS (styled-components), parse a query (graphql-tag's gql), or localise text. The raw, un-escaped strings are also available via strings.raw (what String.raw uses). ### Real-world tags | Library / API | Tag | | --- | --- | | styled-components | styled.div`...` | | GraphQL | gql`...` | | Built-in | String.raw`...` | | Custom | escaping, i18n, logging | > **Interview tip:** The key insight to state: the tag receives strings and values **separately**, so it can treat interpolated data differently from the literal template — that separation is what makes safe escaping and DSLs possible.

examples.label: A safe-highlighting tag function

title: What are template literals?
answer:
**Core concept.** Template literals are strings delimited by **backticks** that add three things over quoted strings: ${...} **interpolation** of any expression, real **multi-line** strings, and freedom from escaping the other quote characters. They're the default way to build strings in modern JavaScript.
**How it works.** Inside ${ } you can put any expression — variables, arithmetic, function calls, ternaries — and its result is stringified into place. Newlines inside the backticks are preserved literally, which makes multi-line HTML/SQL readable without \n + concatenation. A template literal can also be *tagged* with a function for advanced processing (see tagged template literals). ### vs old-style strings | | Quoted strings | Template literals | | --- | --- | --- | | Insert a variable | "Hi " + name | `Hi ${name}` | | Multi-line | "a\n" + "b" | real line breaks | | Embed quotes | escape them | use freely | | Advanced | — | tags, String.raw | > **Interview tip:** Beyond convenience, note ${} accepts *any* expression (e.g. ${a > b ? "hi" : "lo"}), and that template literals underpin tagged templates — the foundation of libraries like styled-components.

examples.label: Interpolation and multi-line

title: What are the basics of regular expressions in JavaScript?
answer:
**Core concept.** A regular expression describes a **text pattern**. In JS you write one as a literal between slashes (/pattern/flags) or with new RegExp(), then use methods like test (boolean), match/matchAll (extract), and replace (substitute). Flags like g (global), i (case-insensitive), and m (multiline) tune the matching.
**How it works.** The pattern combines literal characters with metacharacters: \d (digit), \w (word char), \s (whitespace), . (any), quantifiers + * ? {n,m}, anchors ^ $, character classes [...], and capture groups ( ) referenced in replace as $1. Use a literal /.../ for fixed patterns and new RegExp(str) when the pattern is built dynamically (remembering to double-escape backslashes in the string form). ### Common pieces | Token | Matches | | --- | --- | | \d / \w / \s | digit / word char / whitespace | | + * ? | one-or-more / zero-or-more / optional | | ^ $ | start / end of input (or line with m) | | (abc) | capture group → $1 | > **Interview tip:** Mention the global-flag gotcha: a regex with g is **stateful** (lastIndex advances), so reusing one across test calls can skip matches. Prefer matchAll for all groups, and validate with anchors (^…$) to match the *whole* string.

examples.label: test, match, replace, capture groups

title: What are the quirks of the typeof operator?
answer:
**Core concept.** typeof returns a **string** naming the operand's type. It's handy for primitives, but carries famous quirks: typeof null is "object" (a legacy bug), functions report "function" (not "object"), typeof NaN is "number", and typeof on an **undeclared** variable is safely "undefined" instead of throwing.
**How it works.** typeof reliably distinguishes the primitives ("string", "number", "boolean", "bigint", "symbol", "undefined") and "function". Its blind spot is objects: arrays, dates, null, and plain objects all report "object", so you need Array.isArray() or Object.prototype.toString.call(x) to tell them apart. The "safe on undeclared" behaviour makes typeof x !== "undefined" the classic existence check. ### typeof results | Value | typeof | | --- | --- | | null | "object" ⚠️ | | [], {} | "object" | | function | "function" | | NaN | "number" | | undeclared | "undefined" (no throw) | > **Interview tip:** The headline quirk is typeof null === "object". For precise object-type checks, cite Object.prototype.toString.call(x) (e.g. "[object Array]") and Array.isArray().

examples.label: The quirks, demonstrated
examples.code:
console.log(typeof null);        // "object"   (historical bug)
console.log(typeof function(){}); // "function"
console.log(typeof NaN);          // "number"
console.log(typeof []);           // "object"   → use Array.isArray
console.log(typeof 10n);          // "bigint"
console.log(typeof Symbol());     // "symbol"

// safe on UNDECLARED identifiers (no ReferenceError)
console.log(typeof neverDeclared); // "undefined"

// precise object discrimination
console.log(Object.prototype.toString.call([]));  // "[object Array]"
console.log(Array.isArray([]));                     // true

title: What do Array.from and Array.of do?
answer:
**Core concept.** Both are static array constructors. Array.from(x) builds a real array from an **iterable or array-like** (with an optional map function). Array.of(...items) builds an array from its **arguments** — fixing the confusing new Array(n) behaviour.
**How it works.** Array.from turns anything iterable (strings, Set, Map) or array-like (has length + indices, like arguments or a DOM NodeList) into an array; its second argument is a map function, so Array.from({length: 3}, (_, i) => i) generates [0,1,2]. Array.of sidesteps the new Array(7) quirk where a single number creates an *empty* array of that length instead of [7]. ### Building arrays | Call | Result | | --- | --- | | Array.from("hi") | ["h", "i"] | | Array.from({length:3}, (_,i)=>i) | [0, 1, 2] | | Array.of(7) | [7] | | new Array(7) | empty × 7 ⚠️ | > **Interview tip:** Two useful one-liners: Array.from(new Set(arr)) to dedupe, and Array.from({length:n}, (_,i)=>i) to make a range. Mention spread ([...iterable]) as the terser alternative when you don't need the map callback.

examples.label: Converting and generating
examples.code:
console.log(Array.from("hi"));                 // ["h", "i"]
console.log(Array.from(new Set([1, 1, 2, 3]))); // [1, 2, 3] (dedupe)
console.log(Array.from({ length: 4 }, (_, i) => i * i)); // [0, 1, 4, 9]

console.log(Array.of(7));     // [7]
console.log(new Array(7).length); // 7 — empty slots, NOT [7]

// spread is the terser conversion when no map fn is needed
console.log([...new Set([2, 2, 5])]); // [2, 5]

title: What do Object.keys, values, and entries return?
answer:
**Core concept.** All three take an object and return an **array** of its *own enumerable string-keyed* properties: Object.keys → an array of keys, Object.values → an array of values, and Object.entries → an array of [key, value] pairs. They're the bridge that lets you iterate and transform objects with array methods.
**How it works.** Each returns only the object's **own** (not inherited) **enumerable** properties, with non-symbol keys, in insertion order (integer-like keys sort numerically first). entries pairs beautifully with for…of destructuring and with Object.fromEntries — together they let you map/filter an object as if it were an array and rebuild it. ### The trio | Method | Returns | | --- | --- | | Object.keys(o) | ["a", "b"] | | Object.values(o) | [1, 2] | | Object.entries(o) | [["a",1], ["b",2]] | | Object.fromEntries(pairs) | rebuilds the object | > **Interview tip:** The power combo is Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(obj).map(...)) — transform an object immutably in one line. Note these skip inherited and symbol keys; use Reflect.ownKeys if you need everything.

examples.label: Iterate and transform an object

title: What do flat and flatMap do?
answer:
**Core concept.** flat(depth) returns a new array with nested sub-arrays flattened up to depth levels (default 1; pass Infinity to fully flatten). flatMap(fn) is map followed by a single level of flat — ideal for mapping where each item can expand to zero, one, or many results.
**How it works.** flat only descends the number of levels you ask for, so deeply-nested data needs flat(Infinity). flatMap exists because "map then flatten one level" is so common; it's also slightly more efficient than chaining and lets a callback return [] to **drop** an item or an array to **expand** it — effectively a combined map + filter + expand. ### flat vs flatMap | Call | Result | | --- | --- | | [1,[2,[3]]].flat() | [1, 2, [3]] (1 level) | | [1,[2,[3]]].flat(Infinity) | [1, 2, 3] | | [1,2].flatMap(x => [x, x*10]) | [1, 10, 2, 20] | | [1,2,3].flatMap(x => x % 2 ? [x] : []) | [1, 3] (map + filter) | > **Interview tip:** Note flatMap only flattens **one** level — it won't fully flatten deep results. And the "return [] to drop, return many to expand" trick is the elegant answer for combined transform-and-filter in one pass.

examples.label: Flattening and expanding

title: What does 'use strict' do?
answer:
**Core concept.** "use strict" opts a script or function into **strict mode** — a safer variant of JavaScript that turns previously-silent mistakes into thrown errors and removes confusing legacy behaviour. ES modules and class bodies are strict **automatically**, so you rarely write the directive by hand today.
**How it works.** Place the literal string "use strict"; at the top of a file or function. It then: throws on assignment to an **undeclared** variable; makes this undefined in plain function calls (instead of the global object); forbids duplicate parameter names; throws when writing to read-only/non-writable properties; and bans the legacy with statement. The net effect is fewer accidental globals and earlier, louder errors. ### Sloppy vs strict | Behaviour | Sloppy | Strict | | --- | --- | --- | | Assign to undeclared var | creates a global | ReferenceError | | this in a plain call | global object | undefined | | Duplicate param names | allowed | SyntaxError | | Write to read-only prop | silently ignored | TypeError | > **Interview tip:** The most-quoted effect: a typo'd assignment that *silently created a global* now throws. Add that ES modules and class bodies are strict by default, so the explicit directive is mostly a legacy-script concern.

examples.label: A typo that strict mode catches

title: What does Array.filter do?
answer:
**Core concept.** filter returns a **new array** containing only the elements for which the callback returns a truthy value. It never mutates the original, and the result is the same length or shorter — the idiomatic way to "keep the items that match a condition."
**How it works.** The callback receives (element, index, array) and should return a boolean-ish value; truthy keeps the element, falsy drops it. Because filter returns a fresh array it chains cleanly — arr.filter(...).map(...) — and stays pure, so it's safe in React/state code. To get a single match instead of a sub-list, use find; to collapse to one value, use reduce. ### filter vs its cousins | Method | Returns | | --- | --- | | filter | new array of all matches | | find | the first matching element (or undefined) | | map | new array, same length, transformed | | some | true if any match | > **Interview tip:** Stress purity — filter returns a new array and leaves the source intact, unlike splice. A clean follow-up is removing an item immutably: list.filter(x => x.id !== id).

examples.label: Keep matches, immutably

title: What does Object.create do?
answer:
**Core concept.** Object.create(proto) makes a **new object whose prototype is exactly proto**. It's the most direct expression of prototypal inheritance — you link an object to another object without any constructor or class. A special case, Object.create(null), makes a "bare" object with **no prototype** at all.
**How it works.** Property lookups on the new object fall through to proto via the prototype chain, so methods on proto are shared. An optional second argument supplies property descriptors (like Object.defineProperties). Object.create(null) is handy for dictionaries/lookup maps: with no inherited Object.prototype, there's no toString/hasOwnProperty to collide with user keys — safer than {} for untrusted keys. ### Uses | Call | Result | | --- | --- | | Object.create(proto) | new object inheriting from proto | | Object.create(null) | prototype-less "bare" object | | Object.create(p, descriptors) | + defined properties | > **Interview tip:** Contrast it with {} (which inherits Object.prototype) and with constructor/class inheritance. The standout use case: Object.create(null) as a collision-proof map — no inherited keys to worry about.

examples.label: Linking a prototype + bare dictionary

title: What does setTimeout with 0 delay actually do?
answer:
**Core concept.** setTimeout(fn, 0) does **not** run fn immediately. It queues fn as a **macrotask** that runs only after the current synchronous code finishes *and* the entire microtask queue (promises) has drained. It's a way to "yield" — defer work to the next event-loop turn.
**How it works.** The delay is a **minimum**, not a guarantee — the callback is placed in the macrotask (timer) queue and runs when the call stack is empty and microtasks are done. The HTML spec also clamps nested timeouts to a minimum (~4 ms after several levels). Practical uses: break a long task into chunks so the UI can paint/respond, run code *after* the current call stack unwinds, or escape a deep synchronous flow. For "after the current microtasks but ASAP," queueMicrotask is more precise. ### Scheduling helpers | Call | Runs | | --- | --- | | sync code | immediately | | queueMicrotask / Promise.then | after sync, before timers | | setTimeout(fn, 0) | next macrotask (after microtasks) | | requestAnimationFrame | before the next repaint | > **Interview tip:** Emphasise it's deferral, not immediacy — it always runs **after** the current sync code and **all** pending microtasks. Mention the ~4 ms nested-timeout clamp and that queueMicrotask is the finer-grained "soon."

examples.label: 0ms still runs last

title: What does the delete operator do?
answer:
**Core concept.** delete removes a **property** from an object and returns true on success. That's its whole job — it does **not** delete variables, functions, or free memory, and on an array it removes the element but leaves a *hole* (the length doesn't change).
**How it works.** delete obj.key unbinds that key entirely — afterwards "key" in obj is false (different from merely setting it to undefined). It can't remove var/let/const bindings or function declarations (returns false, or throws in strict mode). On arrays it creates a sparse "empty" slot rather than shifting elements, so for real removal use splice, filter, or set to undefined deliberately. ### delete behaviour | Target | Result | | --- | --- | | delete obj.prop | removes the key, returns true | | delete arr[i] | leaves a hole, length unchanged | | delete variable | false / strict SyntaxError | | set to undefined | key **stays**, value is undefined | > **Interview tip:** Two points score well: (1) delete on an array leaves a sparse hole — use splice/filter for true removal; (2) deleting a property is *not* the same as setting it to undefined — only delete makes in / hasOwnProperty report false.

examples.label: Property vs array behaviour

title: What does yield* do in a generator?
answer:
**Core concept.** yield* *delegates* to another iterable from inside a generator. Instead of yielding a single value, it yields **every** value the target produces, one by one — flattening a sub-sequence into the current generator's output.
**How it works.** yield expr produces one value; yield* iterable hands control to that iterable and re-yields each of its values until it's exhausted, then resumes. It works on **any** iterable — another generator, an array, a string, a Set. It also forwards the delegated generator's **return value** as the result of the yield* expression, which makes it the natural tool for composing or recursively walking generators (e.g. tree traversal). ### yield vs yield* | | yield x | yield* iterable | | --- | --- | --- | | Produces | one value (x) | every value of iterable | | Operand | any expression | an iterable | | Use | emit a single item | delegate / flatten a sub-sequence | > **Interview tip:** Frame yield* as "generator composition." A clean example is recursive tree traversal — a generator that yield*s itself on each child node yields the whole tree as a flat stream.

examples.label: Delegating to generators and arrays

title: What is BigInt?
answer:
**Core concept.** BigInt is a primitive for **arbitrary-precision integers** — whole numbers larger than Number can safely hold. Regular numbers are 64-bit floats and lose precision past Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (2⁵³−1); BigInt has no such limit. Create one with an n suffix (10n) or BigInt(10).
**How it works.** BigInt arithmetic stays exact no matter how large the value, which matters for IDs, timestamps in nanoseconds, cryptography, and big counters. The catch: you **can't mix** BigInt and Number in an operation (10n + 1 throws a TypeError) — convert explicitly first. BigInt is integer-only (no decimals), typeof 10n is "bigint", and it isn't supported by JSON.stringify. ### Number vs BigInt | | Number | BigInt | | --- | --- | --- | | Range | safe to 2⁵³−1 | unlimited integers | | Decimals | yes | no (integers only) | | Literal | 10 | 10n | | Mix in math | — | not with Number | | typeof | "number" | "bigint" | > **Interview tip:** Name the concrete failure: 9007199254740992 === 9007199254740993 is true for plain numbers (precision loss), whereas BigInt keeps them distinct. Mention you must convert before mixing types.

examples.label: Precision past MAX_SAFE_INTEGER

title: What is CORS?
answer:
**Core concept.** CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a **browser** security mechanism that relaxes the Same-Origin Policy in a controlled way. When a page makes a request to a *different* origin, the browser only exposes the response to JavaScript if the **server** opts in with the right Access-Control-Allow-* response headers.
**How it works.** An "origin" is scheme + host + port; a request to a different one is cross-origin. The browser sends the Origin header and checks the response: if Access-Control-Allow-Origin matches (or is *), JS can read it; otherwise the browser blocks access (the request may still hit the server). "Non-simple" requests (e.g. PUT, custom headers) trigger a **preflight** OPTIONS request first, which the server must approve with Allow-Methods/Allow-Headers. Crucially, **CORS is enforced by the browser, not a server-side firewall** — and it doesn't apply to server-to-server calls. ### Key headers | Header | Purpose | | --- | --- | | Access-Control-Allow-Origin | which origin(s) may read the response | | Access-Control-Allow-Methods | allowed methods (preflight) | | Access-Control-Allow-Headers | allowed request headers (preflight) | | Access-Control-Allow-Credentials | allow cookies/auth | > **Interview tip:** Two points score well: CORS is a **browser** policy (a curl/Postman call ignores it), and the **server** — not the client — fixes a CORS error by sending the right headers. Mention the preflight OPTIONS for non-simple requests.

examples.label: Cross-origin fetch + the headers that unblock it

title: What is JSON and why is it commonly used?
answer:
**Core concept.** JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight, **text-based** data format for exchanging structured data. It's language-independent and human-readable, which is why it's the default for APIs, config files, and storage. In JS you serialize with JSON.stringify and parse with JSON.parse.
**How it works.** JSON supports a small set of types: objects, arrays, strings, numbers, booleans, and null. JSON.stringify walks a value and emits text (with optional replacer and indentation args); JSON.parse does the reverse (with an optional reviver). Know the lossy edges: functions, undefined, and symbols are **dropped** (or become null in arrays), Date objects become ISO **strings**, Map/Set don't serialize, BigInt throws, and **circular references** throw a TypeError. ### Gotchas to know | Input | After stringify → parse | | --- | --- | | function / undefined | dropped from objects | | Date | ISO string (not a Date) | | Map / Set | {} (not serialized) | | circular reference | TypeError | > **Interview tip:** A favourite quick gotcha: JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) is a common "deep clone," but it silently loses functions/undefined/dates and dies on cycles — call out structuredClone() as the safer modern alternative.

examples.label: Serialize, parse, and the lossy edges

title: What is a higher-order function?
answer:
**Core concept.** A *higher-order function* (HOF) is a function that does at least one of two things: it **takes another function as an argument**, or it **returns a function**. They are possible because functions in JavaScript are first-class values — you can pass them around like any other data.
**How it works.** Because a function value can be stored, passed, and returned, you can build small composable behaviours. Array.prototype.map, filter, and reduce are HOFs that take a callback. Returning a function lets you **pre-bake configuration** — the basis of function factories, currying, decorators (logging/memoizing wrappers), and partial application. ### Built-in HOFs you'll use daily | HOF | Takes | Returns | | --- | --- | --- | | map | transform fn | a new array | | filter | predicate fn | a new (shorter) array | | reduce | reducer fn | a single accumulated value | | sort | comparator fn | the sorted array | > **Interview tip:** Tie HOFs to "functions are first-class citizens." A strong follow-up answer shows a function that *returns* a function (e.g. a withLogging wrapper or a multiplier factory), not just one that takes a callback.

examples.label: Taking and returning functions

title: What is a pure function?
answer:
**Core concept.** A *pure function* satisfies two rules: **(1)** given the same inputs it always returns the same output (deterministic), and **(2)** it produces **no side effects** — it doesn't mutate external state, touch the DOM, log, call an API, or read changing globals. Its output depends only on its arguments.
**How it works.** Purity makes code **referentially transparent** — a call can be replaced by its result without changing behaviour. That is what makes pure functions trivial to test (no mocks, no setup), safe to cache/memoize, easy to run in parallel, and the reason React reducers and selectors are expected to be pure. Side-effecting work isn't bad — you just push it to the edges and keep the core logic pure. ### Pure vs impure | | Pure | Impure | | --- | --- | --- | | Same input → same output | always | not guaranteed | | Mutates arguments / globals | never | often | | Side effects (DOM, I/O, log) | none | yes | | Testability | trivial | needs mocks/setup | > **Interview tip:** Call out that array methods split along this line — map/filter/slice are pure (return new arrays) while push/splice/sort mutate in place. Preferring the pure ones avoids a whole class of shared-state bugs.

examples.label: Pure vs impure side by side

title: What is an IIFE?
answer:
**Core concept.** An IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression) is a function that's **defined and called at once**: (function(){ ... })(). Its purpose is to create a **private scope** so variables inside don't leak to the surrounding (historically global) scope — the classic pre-modules encapsulation trick.
**How it works.** Wrapping the function in parentheses turns a function *declaration* into an *expression*, and the trailing () invokes it immediately. Everything declared inside is scoped to that call and disappears afterward (unless returned via a closure). Before ES modules and let/const, IIFEs were the standard way to avoid polluting the global namespace, build the "module pattern" (return a public API while keeping internals private), and capture per-iteration values in var loops. Today block scope and modules cover most of these needs. ### Why use one | Goal | How | | --- | --- | | Avoid global pollution | private scope | | Module pattern | return a public API object | | Run setup once | execute immediately | | Async top-level (pre-TLA) | (async () => { ... })() | > **Interview tip:** Note IIFEs are *mostly* superseded by ES modules + block scope, but still appear as (async () => {})() to use await where top-level await isn't available, and in bundled/minified output.

examples.label: Private scope + the module pattern

title: What is debouncing and throttling?
answer:
**Core Concept (TL;DR)** Debouncing and throttling are optimization techniques used to limit the execution rate of a function responding to high-frequency events. Debouncing delays execution until a quiet period occurs, while throttling limits execution to once per time interval.
**Under-the-Hood Mechanics** - **Debounce**: - Starts a timer on each event trigger. - If a new event fires before the timer completes, the previous timer is cancelled, and a new one starts. - The function only executes once the timer completes. - Excellent for input validation, search inputs, and auto-save fields. - **Throttle**: - Sets a block window once executed. - Ignores or buffers intermediate event triggers until the block window expires. - Guarantees execution at a regular interval. - Excellent for scroll event trackers, resize layouts, and drag-and-drop handles. **Tricky Code Scenarios & Gotchas** - **Losing Closure State**: Debounce/throttle wrappers rely on closures to maintain timer states (`timeoutId` or `lastRunTime`). ```javascript // Bug: Instantiating the wrapper inside a React functional component render loop creates a new closure on every render! // Fix: Wrap the debounced function in a hook (e.g., useCallback or useRef) to persist the closure. ``` **Architectural Rationale** These optimization strategies reduce execution pressure on CPU, memory, and networking pipelines, preventing application lag and eliminating server-side rate limit errors (like HTTP 429). **Pro-Level Interview Tip** Illustrate how debouncing helps search auto-suggest fields, and how throttling prevents performance lags on layout scroll event listeners.

examples.label: debounce vs throttle behavior
